cards.title: AI's Next Wave: Shipping Faster, But Keeping Your Secrets Safe
cards.thesis: Mega-powerful AI models like the rumored GPT-5.6 Sol Ultra are on the way, promising to change how everyone codes. But builders are getting super paranoid that big tech companies are using their code and ideas to train these AIs and gain an unfair advantage, creating a massive trust gap. This means there's a huge opportunity for AI tools that help people build and ship quickly, but with rock-solid privacy and control over their intellectual property (their unique ideas and code).

evidence.text: GPT-5.6 Sol Ultra will be in Codex, signaling a huge leap in AI coding capabilities.

People are very paranoid about sharing potential AI research with big AI agents like Claude or ChatGPT/Codex, believing these companies are 'paperclip optimizers' that will use their data to squash competition.
One builder shared, 'I can build anything, but only the void sees it,' having launched 10+ ideas without users, highlighting the struggle to get noticed even when shipping a lot.

Developers are finding themselves 'writing less and less code, but reading way more than before' thanks to AI, indicating a shift in how they interact with their codebase and a need for different kinds of tools.

cards.opportunity: Builders are getting paranoid about giving their secret sauce (IP) to big AI companies, especially with new super-powerful models like GPT-5.6 on the horizon. The gap isn't just 'private AI,' it's giving vibe coders an AI co-pilot that *guarantees* their IP stays theirs while helping them ship faster. Imagine a 'local-only' AI agent that integrates with their existing tools (like Replit or Cursor), promising zero data leaves their machine, letting them code like crazy without fear.

cards.title: Gamers are OBSESSED with 'Premium Performance' — Can You Give Them an Edge Legally?
cards.thesis: Forget 'cheats' — competitive gamers are actively searching for tools that promise a 'premium performance utility' and 'hero optimization.' These GitHub projects, despite being hacks, show a strong demand for anything that gives players a competitive edge, even if they have to dig for it.

evidence.text: An 'Advanced Marvel Rivals Internal Suite' offering 'Premium Performance Utility with Aim Assist, ESP, and Hero Optimization' is gaining traction.
A 'WarFrame Menu' promising 'aim assistance,' 'recoil compensation,' 'configurable wallhacks,' and 'ESP' has significant engagement, showing a clear market for tools that provide in-game advantages.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's looking for an edge in competitive games, and while these GitHub repos are for cheats, they highlight a massive market for 'premium performance utilities' and 'hero optimization.' You could build a subscription service or a marketplace for legitimate, developer-approved 'pro-player' overlays and companion apps for popular competitive games (like Valorant, Apex, or the upcoming Marvel Rivals) — focus on features like advanced stat tracking, strategic insights, or custom UI elements that give a real advantage without breaking game rules, tapping into the same desire for an 'internal suite' but ethically.
